Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.Doi Number A Maturity Model for Digital Product Passports: A Design Science Study Dimitri Petrik1, Fabian Dzierzawa2, and Kirsten Warthmann2 1Graduate School of Excellence advanced Manufacturing Engineering (GSaME), University of Stuttgart, Stuttgart 70569 Germany 2 Department VIII: Information Systems II, University of Stuttgart, Stuttgart 70174 Germany Corresponding author: Dimitri Petrik (e-mail: dimitri.petrik@gsame.uni-stuttgart.de). This work was supported in part by the Ministry of Science, Research and the Arts of the State of Baden-Wurttemberg within the sustainability support of the projects of the Exzellenzinitiative II. ABSTRACT As part of the Green Deal announced by the European Commission, digital product passports (DPPs) are becoming increasingly important for companies to become more sustainable. DPPs are an emerging concept for structuring sustainability-related product data and making it fit for inter-organizational data sharing. As such, DPPs hold promise to close information gaps in supply chain and production networks and support sustainable paradigms such as the circular economy. As DPPs are intended to make data available for third parties, their launch requires industrial organizations to develop certain information technology (IT) and data management capabilities. In addition, the solution space for implementing DPPs is vast and is still only determined by vague legislation. These factors create uncertainty for companies that need to launch and operate DPPs. Initial research projects indicate an equifinality in the design of DPPs and do not focus on the processes to integrate the data in DPPs and operate them successfully. To assist industrial organizations in this complex endeavor, this study employs design science research to propose a validated maturity model. The model integrates nine technological and organizational maturation paths that industrial organizations can use to become conscious of their current state and improve systematically to launch and operate DPPs successfully. The knowledge base was derived from a multivocal literature review (MLR) and refined in two series of interviews with experts from industrial organizations. Between the two series of interviews, the maturity model was instantiated as a transfer media. The model enables decision-makers and researchers to assess a systematic optimization of technological and organizational maturation paths in order to turn DPPs into a competitive advantage by making more sustainable decisions. INDEX TERMS Digital Product Passports, Data Management, Maturity Model, Supply Chain Traceability, Circular Economy I. INTRODUCTION If we use resources at the rate of the most resource-intensive nation, we will exhaust the planet’s annual resource budget for 2025 by the 6 th of February. Even at the average global rate of consumption in 2024, humanity would have exhausted the Earth's resources by August, living on ecological credit for the remaining months [1]. To address this problem, the circular economy (CE) is a promising approach to tackling the problem of resource consumption. Acknowledging the multitude of existing definitions, CE can be defined as a “system that enables resource efficiency by minimizing waste and maximizing the reuse and recycling of materials” [2]. Although CE holds promise to extend product lifecycles and keep resources in the loop significantly longer than the traditional “take-make-waste” paradigm, its realization in the industrial practice lags behind [3]. A pertinent issue that hampers the realization of CE is the lack of available data and the resulting information gaps, especially when it comes to managing value streams within and across supply chains. A lack of transparency due to insufficient data exchange and a lack of standardization are currently the biggest obstacles to the circular economy that need to be overcome [4], [5]. Only then can informed decisions be made about product design, production, as well as reuse and recycling [6]. Thus, tracking products or materials and managing their return to minimize resource consumption can greatly benefit from data-driven decisions [7]. The importance of data in realizing a CE goes in line with the changing role of data, which has evolved from being an influential resource in the 1990s to becoming a critical driver This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. 4 VOLUME XX, 2017 of competitive advantage [8]. Data provides the essential foundation for informed decisions, although data management remains a challenge in industrial practice [9]. Driven by political initiatives, such as the Green Deal, and its key components, such as the Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR), Digital Product Passports (DPPs) have emerged as a novel concept to facilitate inter-organizational sharing of product-related data to foster sustainable decision- making [10]. These legal regulations mandate that DPPs become mandatory for economic operators in certain domains, including iron and steel, aluminum, textiles (including garments and footwear), furniture (including mattresses), tires, detergents, paints, lubricants, chemicals, energy-related products (including batteries), ICT products and consumer electronics [10]. As a result, DPPs must be created and maintained for each new product being manufactured and placed on the European market [10], [11]. The European Commission, as well as the voices from the industry, consider DPPs to be a purposeful means for accumulating useful data throughout the product lifecycle and making it available to all stakeholders involved in the assembly, sale, and (re)use of the product [12], [13]. As we know from research on inter-organizational data sharing, numerous socio-technical factors influence the realization of value from data [14], [15]. Given these factors, implementing DPPs requires organizations to routinely manage data pipelines, ensure data quality, and make data available to external stakeholders [16], [17]. In this context, the complexity for industrial organizations arises from the different existing data management paradigms. While industrial incumbents with their legacy IT systems developed decentralized structures that serve as data silos, it is today possible to transform them into centralized structures, such as enterprise-wide data lakes. Although this transition promotes the dissolution of data silos and the democratization of data, it can become a bottleneck because it lacks any context-based responsibility related to the data being processed, and limits all resources to those that belong to the centralized solution [18]. Other paradigms have emerged to address these data management issues, such as the data mesh, which shifts the responsibility to a decentralized domain-oriented data architecture where data owners manage, use and provide the data. However, with few exceptions [19], [20], limited knowledge exists on how these paradigms can be implemented in industrial companies, resulting in a formidable challenge for industrial companies to launch and operate DPPs successfully. Despite the lucrative promises of improving sustainable performance [6], implementing and operating DPPs throughout the product lifecycle remains a pertinent challenge for industrial companies [11], [21]. Previous research on DPPs is still in its infancy and has only collected a few design principles for implementing DPPs to promote trust in data sharing between companies [11], [22], as well as initial requirements for technical realization [23] and relevant data categories [17], [24], [25]. While these papers mark a valuable starting point, they lack a perspective on maturation paths between an initial launch and the improvement of a DPP and the data management behind it. The difficulty of obtaining the relevant data varies, as does the effort required to establish the numerous use cases based on the DPP data [25], [26]. Thus, there are still many open issues in the design of a DPP and the capabilities needed to manage it, for which only initial proposals can be found in the literature [24]. Considering that economic operators in certain industries will soon be required by law to launch and operate DPPs, the insufficient knowledge base motivated us to create a maturity model, as they can help decision-makers measure performance and understand what and how to improve in different ways [27]. Despite the existence of a few maturity models for CE [28], there is only one model so far that deals with data [29]. Despite the growing interest in providing relevant data on the sustainability of DPPs, and even a legal obligation to launch them in certain domains, there are currently no maturity models that transparently present the capabilities linked to their introduction and map their systematic improvement. Our maturity model helps to understand the possible options for implementing DPPs in an industrial organization. We consider a maturity model as an assessment tool to support industrial organizations in launching DPPs and reflecting on structured steps for improvement in the course of transformation towards smart CE [7], [30]. The lack of maturity models in research in practices for DPPs as enablers of smart CE, leads to the following research question: How can a maturity model be designed to support organizations in launching and operating Digital Product Passports? To answer this question, this study employs the design science research (DSR) paradigm, following the iterative DSR methodology (DSRM) proposed by Peffers and colleagues [31] to create an artifact with a strong practical relevance [32]. This artifact is a maturity model that aims to support industrial organizations in managing the organizational change associated with a successful launch and operation of DPPs. It consists of different dimensions with different maturation paths, divided into different levels [33]. Following guidelines from Becker and colleagues [34] for rigorous development of maturity models, we performed iterative maturity model development. It included 1) a multivocal literature review (MLR) to assess the existing knowledge base on DPPs and derive a preliminary structure of a maturity model; 2) a consultation of industry experts to refine the maturity model; 3) an instantiation of a model as a prototype and 4) a subsequent evaluation of the maturity model’s usefulness as well as its final refinement. The structure of this paper is organized as follows. Sec. II introduces key concepts, including DPPs, data management, and maturity models. Sect. III details the research design. Sect. IV presents the maturity model for DPPs. Sect. V describes the model refinements from the empirical evaluation. Sect. VI This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. VIII provides the concluding remarks. II. RELATED CONCEPTS A. DIGITAL PRODUCT PASSPORTS Under the Green Deal, the European Commission published a series of proposals on the 30 th of March 2022 to increase the resource independence of the EU and establish sustainable products as the standard, with the DPP included among these measures. Following these proposals, the ESPR entered into force on the 18 th of July 2024, thus incorporating the DPP [35]. For certain domains, such as automotive, the upcoming regulatory directives will require companies with direct contact with the end customer – so-called economic operators – to launch and operate domain-specific instances of a DPP. Looking inside the battery regulation, which contains regulatory requirements for the battery passport instance of a DPP, an automobile manufacturer with electric vehicles in its product portfolio will have to ensure the traceability of the traction batteries in the field and provide information on the carbon intensity of the manufacturing processes or a bill of materials [36]. Such data is considered essential for the realization of end-to-end tracking and the efficient implementation of the different CE strategies, although recent studies show that DPPs also act as purposeful data vessels for other use cases, such as compliance with social standards or carbon accounting [26], [37]. Numerous definitions of the DPP have been offered by policy institutions, practitioners, and academics [38]. In the ESPR, the DPP is defined as a product-specific dataset containing data related to that product made available via a data carrier [13]. The information within the dataset may vary depending on regulatory requirements. Initial research projects show that DPPs can purposefully incorporate information on the components, chemical composition and origin of materials, repairability options, spare part services, disassembly instructions, as well as the information relevant to carbon accounting, such as the carbon footprint [24], [37]. Consequently, the data model of a DPP may also mature over time, which is related to the fact that certain data can be difficult to obtain [25]. Hence, a DPP can be thought of as an extensible knowledge graph [39]. The EU Battery Regulation, for instance, specifies that the battery passport should include around 90 mandatory data attributes for traction batteries with capacities of over two kWh in electric vehicles and light transport vehicles, such as E-Bikes [12], [36]. Despite the fact that batteries are already constantly produced, it is currently unclear which specific data points a DPP must contain for each respective product group, as work on the legal acts is still ongoing, and the first work plans are not expected until the second quarter of 2025. However, industries with products that are not affected by the legislation are also starting to show interest in DPPs [40, c.f.] for conceptualizing a DPP for combustion engines). It is therefore expected that data models and standards for DPPs will vary from industry to industry. However, the data of a comprehensive DPP data model is distributed across different stages of the supply chain. Consequently, the implementation of a DPP depends on inter-organizational data sharing [41], [22]. Thus, DPPs represent the state-of-the-art instantiable socio-technical systems in the constantly evolving landscape of information systems (IS) for the systematic collection, storage, and processing of product-related data [16]. From a technical perspective, it is not just about the data carrier (e.g., a QR code), which the end customer can scan to obtain information from a single record [42]. In addition to the carrier, the implementation of a DPP requires a backend system consisting of several building blocks [43], as depicted in Fig. 1. A DPP can also benefit from a digital twin, which can contain a more comprehensive data model of the product and the entire system around it, especially for complex products, so that a digital twin can act as a data source for the DPP [6], [44]. The individual data record, which may relate to a specific product or batch of products with a low economic value like packaging materials, and is enriched with data over the product life cycle, relates to a data model. With a new life cycle, the stakeholders (i.e., a new product owner) can change, and the data accesses can change accordingly. For instance, additional life cycles are expected for batteries so that they can continue to be used as stationary energy storage devices before they are disassembled and recycled [45]. To create DPPs and manage their changes, for instance, when the product is decommissioned, a backend system with sufficient capabilities will be required. We refer to such backend systems as data platforms and introduce them in the next section, describing the specifics of the data management they help to achieve. B. DATA PLATFORMS AND MANAGEMENT Seamless interaction between the data carrier and the data model of a DPP, its secure storage with simultaneous provision of different access points for other actors in the supply chain at different tiers and end customers, as well as the analysis of data from the DPP to optimize one’s own processes, requires advanced data management [17]. Data management describes paradigms on how to organize the treatment of data within a structured context [46]. To cope with the data-related challenges of DPPs, we consider the main paradigms of data management: centralized monolithic data architecture or decentralized and distributed one. From the historical problem of data silos caused by isolated solutions from data consumers within an organization, the centralized paradigm has occurred, promoting data democracy within organizations by storing all data centrally and making it accessible to relevant stakeholders [47], [48]. 4 VOLUME XX, 2017 Figure 1. Conceptual view on Digital Product Passports To realize data management, data platforms are needed to manage DPPs and to map new business processes [43], [49]. Data platforms focus on technical implementations to ensure data sharing [9]. In terms of DPPs, these systems will need to ensure interoperability [50] while implementing trusted and flexible data governance for inter-enterprise data sharing, to be defined by the manufacturing companies [16], [22], [51]. Fig. 1 provides a conceptual view of DPPs and the data platforms for their management and shows that data platforms can create the DPPs, store the data, perform data mapping, and provide external access to work with or update a DPP. A data platform focuses on the technical implementation of the data architecture. [9] adds that a data platform is a choice between a data lake, a data lakehouse, or a data marketplace. Data marketplaces are out of our scope because the ESPR restricts the sale of data from DPPs by digital service providers (i.e., organizations operating a data marketplace) [10]. Thus, a data lake offers a similar idea by not only being a self-service platform but also bundling data provision to a centralized resource that acts as an intermediary between data consumers and data producers. While siloed solutions, such as relational databases for storing financial data in spreadsheets, were designed for specific use cases and data structures, a data lake is a data storage technique that can store any data format in parallel with its raw format. This enables data consumers to use all data within an organization, fostering data sharing, breaking down data silos, and increasing value creation based on organizational data [52]. According to the regulations, the digitalization of product- related information is of great importance to support informed decisions on sustainability and to enable a CE within and across supply chains. Therefore, the actors directly involved in the DPP include manufacturers, suppliers, recyclers, as well as the consumers who buy the product. In addition, repair service providers and authorities are also involved in the life cycle and, therefore, have an interest in the DPP [11], [53]. If we think of individual organizations along the supply chain as data silos, a data platform can facilitate both the provision of data to the DPP and the flexible provision of that data to the supply chain actors. We, therefore, argue that the adoption of DPPs to share product-related data across organizational boundaries can benefit significantly from the capabilities of data platforms. However, the introduction of DPPs for different products and materials is expected to generate large amounts of data. Targeted collection and robust provision of this data highlight the complexity of data management for the successful operation of DPPs. Therefore, to ensure the effective and efficient use of DPPs, a number of requirements need to be met, demanding effective IT and data management by the company releasing the DPP [17]. The complexity and dynamism of global supply chains spanning different geographical regions and the complexity of standardization are among the main drivers of complexity in the launch and operation of DPPs [54], advocating for a systematic increase in maturity not only in the architecture of a DPP, but also in data management. C. MATURITY MODELS Increasing effectiveness and efficiency has always been a key objective for companies. The continuous improvement of performance is of central importance [55]. In order to improve processes within an organization, it is essential to determine the current situation. This assessment, also known as the current states, serves to analyze in detail both the internal capabilities of the organization and the quality of its service provision. In practice, however, an objective assessment often proves to be challenging, as there is no defined goal, and it is therefore unclear what data needs to be measured and compared [34]. A maturity model can help to accomplish such a task, as its main purpose is to assess and demonstrate an anticipated path of improvement regarding a specific subject for a defined target group [56]. A maturity model is a reference model that deals with the development states of organizations and the associated use of technologies. It usually consists of a sequence of maturity levels for a class of objects. # Page 5^page=5
4 VOLUME XX, 2017 organizations to assess the as-is-situation, reflect on improvement measures, and control progress [57]. Therefore, they can be assessed from a performance or a lifecycle perspective [58] and be used by practitioners as supportive tools for objective assessment and improvement of products, services, as well as related processes or capabilities [34], [59]. In practice, maturity models create multiple sources of value [55]. Typical benefits include self-assessment, identification of future steps for systematic improvement, and internal or external benchmarking [59]. In theory, maturity models provide the ability to delineate different stages of development, they also represent theories of stage-based evolution [60]. Starting with an initial assessment of the policy regulations, we noticed that the policy allows for technological openness in the implementation of the DPPs [10], which leads to a large solution space. Yet, a so-called “open texture of law” leads to uncertainty related to the implementation of DPPs [61]. For instance, although some providers advertise the protection of data in a DPP as being solved by the immutability of the blockchain [51], the use of blockchain may violate data sovereignty, as the data is distributed across all nodes of a blockchain network This limits any organization’s exclusive control over its usage and access. In line with this design tension, we have sensed concerns about the legally compliant design of the DPPs in dialogue with experts from different industries. This exchange has led us to consider the development of a maturity model for DPPs. Given these design tensions, we follow with the theoretical considerations for maturity models in [30] and reason the equifinality in the design and management of DPPs. Even if the different configurations are legally compliant, they may differ in quality and also influence the confidence of the other actors in a supply chain to share the data using the DPP [22], [54]. Hence, applying the configurational theoretical view of DPP implementations, we argue that a maturity model for DPPs can support informed decisions on the different pathways, especially for manufacturing companies affected by the ESPR. III. RESEARCH METHODOLOGY This section outlines the methodology used to develop the maturity model, which follows the design science research model [31], and the complementary process for rigorously developing maturity models [34], which consisted of three design cycles shown in Fig. 4. A. MODEL CONCEPTUALISATION As described in Section II, we see two major problems that justify the development of a maturity model. First, the solution space for implementing DPPs is vast and due to vague legal requirements [10], and there are different ways to meet the legal requirements. Second, the nascent research on DPPs has not yet considered data management and the design of an adequately powerful data platform for supplying a DPP with data and simultaneously making it available to globally distributed supply chains in an integrated manner. Therefore, we argue that a maturity model can help decision-makers to implement DPPs of sufficient quality to stimulate supply chain partners to collaborate and generate sustainable added value beyond the legal minimum. Figure 2. Research process based on Becker et al. (2009) Existing research suggests that it may be appropriate to develop a maturity model from a conceptual design [34], [58]. In line with this recommendation, in design cycle 1, we performed a careful review of the existing academic literature. Due to the political origins and practical relevance of DPPs, we also decided to include non-academic sources in the sample (i.e., grey literature), which is consistent with the electronic document research method [62]. This decision allowed us to create an inclusive literature sample, and, in particular, helped us to systematically include publications and electronic resources from several funded research projects, such as the CIRPASS Battery Pass project. The combination of academic and non-academic sources results in a multivocal literature review (MLR) [63]. For the academic literature, we followed the guidelines proposed by [64]. First, we systematically searched academic literature via different databases using an open search string “digital product passport*”. The search was performed in June 2024 with the filters set to include hits based on titles, abstracts, and keywords. The following nine databases were searched: EBSCOhost, Web of Science, Science Direct, IEEE Explore, ACM, SpringerLink, Taylor&Francis, Wiley, and AISeL. At the end of the study, the search was repeated in Problem definition Comparison of existing maturity models Selection of a conceptual development strategy Implementation of a transfer media to instantiate the model Multivocal literature review Interviews with industry experts Initial maturity model Refined maturity model Final maturity model Presentation of the transfer media to industry experts This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. We applied the PRISMA guidelines to support the literature screening and filtering process and to minimize the sampling bias [65]. The PRISMA statement in Fig. 3 has two sides. The left side shows the remaining sample size after each stage of filtering. The right side shows the reasons for excluding at each stage. Due to the topic’s novelty, we included conference papers in the sample but excluded preprints that had not undergone peer review. After eliminating the duplicates and other non-relevant sources, the full texts were assessed and compared in discussions within the research team to create consensus on the inclusion of certain sources and their interpretation. After an independent reading of the papers, the final sample consisted of 47 scientific papers. A backward search added six more papers to the sample, and the repeated search to include the newest publications yielded four additional papers, increasing the final sample to 57 papers. The non-academic sources were discovered using the Google search engine using the same search query “digital product passport*” in November 2024. The search yielded 334 potential sites, including many links to the subsites of overarching projects (i.e., distinctive PDF documents from the same research project). Thus, after pre-screening the “grey” literature and performing a qualitative content analysis for inductive category building [66] and a deductive search for technical requirements, design principles, lessons learned, and data management capabilities [64], 58 additional electronic documents were added to the sample. During the content analysis, electronic documents that did not provide any detailed contribution or new insights compared to the academic literature or mixed the notion of DPPs with digital twins were excluded. Table A1 provides an overview of the full sample. During the initial assessment of the existing academic literature and practitioner sources, we ensured that there were no suitable and easily adaptable maturity models for implementing DPPs. We came to this conclusion in particular because even the sources that list requirements or design principles lack the capability view and focus more on the architecture of DPPs without looking more closely at the required data management. On the other hand, although there are maturity models for digital twins [67], we consider them to be a different concept from DPPs, as they do not only contain product data, and as functional models are intended for product engineering and simulations and not for the CE or legal-compliant carbon accounting [26], [44]. This was necessary to justify a conceptual approach for developing a maturity model. The initial model was then developed iteratively. For this purpose, the aforementioned sample was used to collect important details concerning the design, launch, and management of DPPs, where maturation paths were apparent. This information was treated as preliminary design knowledge Figure 3. Literature selection based on PRISMA guidelines and consolidated into dimensions of the initial maturity model. These initial dimensions were then critically discussed within the research team and in the natural environment of the researchers with colleagues from the research department, with knowledge of circular economy, data management, and requirements engineering. The first iteration resulted in three dimensions: data supply, data management, and supply chain integration. As shown in Fig. 4, each dimension incorporates several dimensions derived from the literature. Given the topic’s novelty, this limitation of the existing literature made it clear that further refinement of the maturity model by experts from the field is necessary. For instance, most of the data topics in the literature were more related to the design of the DPPs than to data management. Hence, following the requirement for the conception of transfer and evaluation [24], we proceeded with an empirical evaluation of the maturity model after creating an initial knowledge base. B. MODEL EVALUATION AND REFINEMENT For design cycles 2 and 3, we searched for industry experts on the professional social network LinkedIn. LinkedIn is the largest professional social network, with around 850 million members from 200 countries. We used the same keywords as for the literature, “digital product passport*” and “battery passport*” to search for persons that were engaged either in the launch of DPPs for their employer (i.e., an industrial company or in the development of DPPs (i.e., professional IT service providers that intend to provide DPP implementation and hosting services), or represented industry associations that did pioneer work on communication and educated the association members about the opportunities and challenges of launching DPPs or were engaged in standardization work. A total of fourteen experts were contacted successfully. It should be noted that the industrial companies (IDs 1, 2, 8, 12 and 13 in Table 1) will introduce the DPPs as users, and the software providers (IDs 5, 9, 10, and 11) will develop the DPP solutions and offer them as a service to the industrial companies. RESULTS In terms of structure, the proposed maturity model for DPPs consists of five stages, with the first stage numbered Level 0 because it represents the stage for organizations in which the DPP has not yet been launched but the decision has been taken to launch the DPP, and initial preparations are underway [11]. From Level 1, the DPP is launched, and the maturation pathways allow for leveraging value beyond the legal requirements. The model comprises three dimensions: data supply, data management, and supply chain integration, with nine distinctive maturation paths. To ensure that the model can be used by as many different industrial organizations as possible, the maturation paths are designed to operate independently, allowing companies to progress at different rates along each dimension, as proposed by [60]. Table 3 shows the final maturity model for DPPs. A. DATA SUPPLY Data supply incorporates aspects necessary for a holistic data supply of a DPP with the data, either demanded by law or voluntarily agreed by a supply chain or a production network [22], [40]. This dimension consists of four maturation paths: data scope, data ingestion, data standardization, and data storage. Data scope. Since DPPs can be conceptually understood as extensible knowledge graphs, the comprehensiveness of their data models can grow. On Level 0, before launching a DPP, a responsible economic operator should understand and define what types of data are relevant and where this data can be found [11], [23]. Existing research projects show that before the launch, surveying supply chain partners and negotiating the supply of certain data points in a DPP can be beneficial because certain data points may contain intellectual property or other competitive information, which may be worth protecting [69]. On Level 1 the data model only contains the data points demanded by the relevant regulations [11], [70]. Level 2 extends the data model by voluntary data points or categories [12], [44]. For instance, companies performing reforestation activities can report them in a DPP. The inclusion of computed or aggregated data into the DPP determines Level 3. Voluntary sustainability KPIs or certificates can be mentioned here as an example [44], [71]. Level 4 marks the ability to integrate dynamic real-time data, for instance from sensors, into the data model [54], [70]. While higher levels on this path indicate an increased effort to obtain and supply data to the DPP, regulatory requirements - such as those for traction batteries - may necessitate the inclusion of advanced data types from the outset [70]. In such cases, data from higher maturity levels is shifted to Level 1. Data ingestion. The process of capturing data in source systems and ingesting this data in a data platform to supply the DPP is termed data ingestion. It must be defined before launching a DPP to determine the way of contributing data to the DPP across companies’ borders. It can be improved in terms of automation [11], [22], [72]. Level 1 is, therefore, determined by manual data uploads to create a DPP. Level 2 benefits from a partially automated upload of necessary data in batches [43], [72]. Level 3 introduces change data capture (CDC) batches that automatically track changes of the dataset in real-time or near-real-time in a database. Level 4 can be reached when streaming-based uploads and automated data mapping replace batch uploads to make data ingestion in near- real-time and without manual effort [43], [73]. Data stream processing is particularly purposeful in capturing sensor data and integrating it into a DPP [74], [75], [76], [77]. Standardization leads to a common agreement about the data points, formats, and semantics among all stakeholders involved in the product life cycle. It positively impacts the interoperability of a DPP [50]. The process of standardizing data can follow a maturation path, and an initial part of this process is a mutual agreement (i.e., in a supply chain or specific domain) about the data standards, which must be defined before launching a DPP [23], [50], [78]. This is beneficial since a data model of a DPP can be conceptualized as a knowledge graph and extended to include new data points following changes in law or beneficial use cases, and the processes for data standardization can be improved in terms of their maturity. At Level 0, especially in domains without a regulatory obligation to launch DPPs, proprietary data formats can be hard-coded, requiring the DPP system to be changed any time the input data changes. At Level 1, proprietary data formats are changed in favor of generic data formats, but still, they require specific manual mapping steps [11], [23], [49], [79]. At Level 2, the generic data formats can be additionally certified [22]. At Level 3 automatic notifications are implemented that appear when data formats change or new formats are added. At Level 4, a semantic hub for each request with automatic mapping from a central database is implemented as a system complementary to the DPP [11], [80]. Data storage. It is an important decision on how to store the data of a DPP, which impacts the storage solution as a part of a data platform required to manage a DPP [43], [81], [82]. This maturation path starts with a make-or-buy decision, which determines whether an industrial organization hosts a DPP on its own or contracts a third party [49], [53], [84]. We argue that an external hosting pathway can be followed concerning the limitations of enterprise information systems, IT departments, and capabilities for managing data access by third parties [14], [85]. However, to be sovereign and gain better control over the data, in-house hosting can be considered an option. Consequently, a prototype for this can be driven forward at Level 1. At Level 2, an internal system for managing DPPs is launched, but it is not deployed on a scalable infrastructure and is determined by the manual effort for ingesting data and managing DPPs. At Level 3, the internal DPP system is deployed on a scalable infrastructure [75], [79], [89]. It becomes, in parts, automated and scales better by integrating a data lake with a data catalog as a central repository for storing, processing, and searching for large volumes of structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data that can be orchestrated between source systems, analytics and the data model of a DPP. Following this, at Level 4, this scalable system is enhanced by a seamless access to data for third parties, and thus, is advanced towards a data lakehouse with a distinctive layer for metadata and data governance. In this path, progress depends on the company’s IT capabilities and the strategic value it attributes to the DPP. B. DATA MANAGEMENT Data management incorporates processes that help work and manage the data within a DPP [16]. This dimension comprises three maturation paths: integration, access, and control. System integration. The use of data within a DPP depends on the decisions made regarding the options for seamless integration. In certain domains, where the DPP is required by law, regulators define the integration technologies (e.g., the battery passport regulation). However, the integration of the DPP can be improved, by reducing the effort of integrating additional data sources into the data model of a DPP, ultimately leading to a maturation path. At Level 0, decisions need to be made about the data carriers and other interfaces (i.e., the APIs). At Level 1, especially in domains where the DPP is launched voluntarily, a QR code can be used without connecting to a resolver or a remote database, harboring a static dataset. This is only possible if the DPP is not required by the legislator, otherwise this implementation does not meet the regulatory requirements [90]. At Level 2, internal application programming interfaces (APIs) can be deployed to one or a few enterprise systems (e.g., an enterprise resource planning system) [23], [50], [86]. At Level 3, the internal integration of the company hosting a DPP is more advanced. Thus, additional enterprise systems, such as product lifecycle management (PLM) and customer relationship management systems (CRM) [24] are integrated, and concepts such as data mesh facilitate external access for supply chain partners. At Level 4, the external access to the data model in the DPP becomes more open and less restrictive, while ownership over a DPP moves to a consortium model. Achieving advanced maturity levels on this path depends on the company’s internal depth of value creation and position in the supply chain. Access. The process of inter-organizational data sharing can be improved over time. At Level 0, prior to the DPP launch, inter-organizational data sharing is not realized. At Level 1, external supply chain partners and customers have read access to the data without any integrations, resulting in a lack of access to write data and update the dataset in the DPP in an automated manner. Partial integration for the DPP to read the data from the supply chain actors’ source systems can be achieved at Level 2, while write access can be realized, but it requires manual data access handling. The integration between the DPP system and the enterprise systems of the supply chain actors is achieved at Level 3 for all supply chain levels. Write data access is implemented for authorized stakeholders at this level. Finally, a mesh-based approach to data access management is realized at Level 4, as it provides flexible options with an integrated API gateway for automated management of read and write access by supply chain actors [16], [22], [53]. However, achieving a higher level of maturity on this path depends on trust in individual value-added partners and on perceived liability risks. Control. The third maturation path relates to control mechanisms, which can be automated as maturity increases. Before the DPP launch, at Level 0, the control mechanisms must be defined, and employees must be made aware of external data access controls and trained accordingly. At Level 1, the data access controls are random and manual. At Level 2, the controls become systematic and are extended to data quality, but remain manual [16]. At Level 3, data access and quality controls are partially automated [22]. At Level 4, the control of data access and quality is extended towards data validation and becomes fully automated. In addition, the automated control is extended to optimize data quality at partner organizations contributing data to the DPP. C. SUPPLY CHAIN INTEGRATION Supply chain integration, encompasses progress in integrating supply chain actors for benefits unlocked by DPPs [26]. This dimension consists of two maturation paths: supply chain depth and sustainable value creation and capture. Supply chain depth. Achieving sufficient transparency depends on the participation of supply chain stakeholders and their contribution to a DPP data model. Therefore, the integration of different supply chain tiers results in a maturation path, which is supported by the empirical evidence on the supply chain actors differing in their knowledge of a DPP, and their digital maturity [17], [25], [69]. Similarly, the number of data points to capture value may differ between different actors, impacting the complexity of DPP adoption [24], [44]. At Level 0, the company planning launch a DPP should inform and prepare its suppliers for future data-driven collaboration as these companies will need to adapt to increased interoperability and data sharing processes [72], [87]. At Level 1, direct suppliers (tier-1) should be integrated and involved in contributing data to the DPPs [37], [91]. At Level 2, the next level of suppliers (e.g., tier-2 upstream and downstream suppliers) need to be integrated. At Level 3, an entire supply chain is expected to be integrated to contribute data to a DPP [17]. At Level 4, after all supply chain level have been integrated, a supply chain gains the transparency sufficient to improve its operations based on the data from a DPP, marking the transformation to a more flexible and interconnected circular ecosystem [53], [54], [72]. Sustainable value creation and capture. This maturation path describes the growing benefit of sharing data through DPPs and using them for informed sustainable decisions. The benefit for the supply chain actors is value creation. The benefit for the economic operator who launches the DPP is value capture. No benefits are realized before launching a DPP at Level 0, but the supply chain partners are informed about the forthcoming DPP launch [72]. At Level 1, DPPs can help to achieve basic legal compliance (i.e., in domains where a DPP is demanded by law), but it does not yet create sustainability gains [54]. Such a shift, for instance, results in collective participation in carbon footprint calculations for Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) compliance, ultimately increasing the informative value of the carbon footprint since it starts to capture emissions beyond the single organization’s boundaries [37], [54]. At Level 3, it can be expected that the data from the DPP can be used for a sustainable optimization of existing processes. For instance, products can be better remanufactured due to the disassembly instructions, or downcycling can be prevented by knowing the exact composition of the material [6], [26], supporting the realization of a CE. Noteworthy examples are circular business models that can either improve product designs for circularity or be based upon a leasing program for products that are systematically collected via a take-back system for refurbishment and another lease [21], [88]. V. DEMONSTRATION We developed a web-based application to instantiate the maturity model as a prototype and demonstrate its technical feasibility for self-assessments that can be supported by software and integrated into the working environment of a decision-maker in an industrial organization. It was developed based on the expert feedback from the second iteration. The prototype follows the recommendation of [34] and represents the implementation of a transfer media, which can be accessed online [92]. The prototype consists of three views, as shown in Fig. 5. In the first view (“Instructions”), the steps on how to use the maturity model are explained. In the second view (“Self Assessment”), a user can understand the different maturation paths and reflect on which stage his organization is at in the launch and design of a DPP. VI. EVALUATION A. EXPERT INTERVIEWS This section describes how the maturity model was refined during the empirical evaluation. Initially, the model consisted of only four maturity levels, starting with a prototypical implementation of a DPP. Expert #7 argued that it would better represent the transition of industrial organizations towards launching and working with DPPs if the maturity model included a pre-launch level. As a result, Level 0 was added to the model to represent the planning phase, which is characterized by initial considerations regarding the design, points of conflict, e.g., standardization, and negotiations with other actors in the supply chain to mitigate them. The descriptions of the other four levels across all nine maturation paths were also subsequently adjusted. Initially, we did not integrate the growing extent of the data model into the maturity model. Expert #6 drew our attention to the fact that the complexity of the data model goes hand in hand with the number of data points and potential hierarchies. With this in mind, we included the dimension “data scope” as a maturation path in the model. In particular, Expert #6 helped us to define the levels for the data model and, thus, the “data scope” maturation path. He also drew our attention to the fact that the primary target group of the maturity model are the economic operators. It is, therefore, not possible for tier-n suppliers to reach the final level of “data scope”. The same applies to the “data storage” dimension. Expert #14 confirmed that companies are more likely to license a service provider. However, larger economic operators, such as OEMs, are more likely to set up their own data platforms to operate DPPs due to the strategic importance of the data involved and the existence of own data lakes. Although Expert #1 asked why individual data categories [24, c.f.] were not included in the maturity model, we were unable to identify a maturation pathway based on the different data categories. This is because, for certain tangibles, such as batteries, all data categories become mandatory directly from the introduction of the DPP. We have therefore categorized the “data scope” maturation path between legally required and voluntary data and then differentiated according to the complexity of the data availability. The challenge of managing access to a DPP dataset was confirmed by several experts, who helped us to improve the maturation steps. These improvements concern the “access” and “control” paths. Experts #1, #2, #4, and #14 confirmed that these two paths are important prerequisites for building trust in the usefulness of a DPP and for ensuring that qualified, high-quality data is contributed. Experts #10 and #11 refined our maturity levels slightly, for instance, adding data validation as a control mechanism at Level 4. Additionally, in agreement with Expert #4, the audits for “control” were also added. In addition, Experts #8, #10, #11, and #14 also helped us to improve “data access” with an API gateway and to distinguish between the levels of “data standardization”. Initially, the “data ingestion” dimension focused only on the different data carriers available. However, in our exchange with the experts, we were not able to outline an increase in maturity between QR codes, RFID, or other technologies because their integration in a DPP will depend on the specifics of the tangible object. In addition, starting with a static URL as a preliminary stage was described by expert #8 as unrealistic, and the dimension was therefore discarded. However, with the help of Experts #8, #10, and #11, we focused on automation in the “data ingestion” path and added the “data standardization” maturation path to the model. Furthermore, the experts confirmed that data management and the data platform are very important as a backend for a product passport, although they are rarely considered, with a few exceptions [43]. For example, Expert #14 explained that one should be realistic and that a decentralized architecture based mainly on resolvers to the source systems will not work. He believes that although the architectures are not yet final, persistent storage of the DPP data (i.e., a backend data platform) will be essential. This is because, for example, an economic operator does not have direct contact with the tier-n suppliers. If their direct supply chain partners change them, data availability for the DPP would be lost. According to Expert #14, decentralized architecture means that the European Commission will not provide a centralized solution, but the economic operators will have to take care of their own solutions, i.e., make them available and interoperable, fulfilling a decentralized approached to the launch and operation of DPPs. This shortcoming of the initial model was criticized by Experts #1 and #3. Expert #1 missed the socio-technical perspective, particularly the business value of DPP use. Expert #3 urged us to include more sustainability potential in the model, as the model originally had too shallow link to a CE. Therefore, in line with the literature on DPP potential [12], [26], [34], we shifted the dimension towards the sustainable impact of adopting DPPs. All experts confirmed the absence of any existing maturity models tailored to DPPs and recognized the model’s relevance in supporting a structured launch of a DPP. At the time of data collection, they confirmed that they were unaware of any such models. The experts identified added value for SMEs, in particular, as the question of “how do I get there?” was new to them. The experts were positive about the guidance provided by the maturity model in the tabular view of the prototype. The experts also could clearly articulate the benefits of the model. For example, Expert #5 said that: “DPP is the opportunity for companies to finally digitalize their processes and thus save costs and increase productivity. However, this is not seen, and the impression of the European bureaucracy dominates.” Therefore, the model can help industrial organizations view the DPP as an opportunity to improve digitalization. Based on feedback from Expert #8, certain information about the relevant legislation with its timeline and the affected product categories was included in the prototype's first view to indicate the urgency of DPPs for industrial organizations. In terms of presentation, all the experts agreed that the tabular presentation was highly informative as it allowed individual measures to be identified, while the graphical presentation as a radar chart is clear for reporting the progress. For this reason, our prototype received two views on the results, as described in Sect. V. Lastly, almost all the experts found the maturity model easy enough to understand. Only Expert #6 recommended to add detailed explanations on how to use the maturity model, which was taken into account during the design of the prototypical transfer media, and added in the first view. B. VALIDATION WORKSHOPS All three companies have confirmed that they are currently in the preparation phase, meaning they are at Level 0. When asked about the applicability of the model, representatives from the automotive industry said: “Of course, you can look at it this way: we either fulfill a regulation or we fulfill the regulation and create added value. I think you can only embrace the latter if you have control over the process.” Experts from the plastics and automotive industries have said that the regulatory framework is not yet ready, and that the data for all domains except batteries has yet to be finalized: “The regulatory authorities - whether EU or American - have This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. A technology has not yet been agreed upon.” In the case of batteries, sensor data and KPIs are also legally required at launch [70]. The workshop participants stated: “From a security point of view, one of the most demanding things is providing real-time data to the relevant authorities due to the complexity involved. This is something that we have to do from level 1 onwards.” This regulatory requirement must be considered for the battery passports, meaning that the level of data scope only indicates the effort involved in providing the DPP with data. However, according to workshop participants from other domains, the data scope provides a good overview of the complexity involved and reminds companies which data they have and which they must obtain externally. The evaluation workshops revealed that the final stages of maturity relating to data standardization, storage, and access are not desirable in three specific scenarios due to their technical complexity and the effort required for implementation: (1) for SME suppliers, (2) for products with small batch sizes such as specialized machinery and equipment, and (3) for low-value products such as plastic packaging. The need to integrate third parties and grant them automated write access depends on the depth of value creation. Representatives from the mechanical engineering industry noted that full integration is not worthwhile given the relatively small number of external data points from third parties. Similarly, experts from the automotive sector expressed concerns about granting write access to third parties, citing liability risks associated with external data. In this context, however, the experts commended the path dedicated to the maturity of data quality and validation, viewing it as a significant benefit of the model. The usefulness of higher maturity levels in the system integration and access pathways also depends on the complexity of the supply structures: “In the automotive industry, it's clear that they have highly integrated supply chains. In general industries, it is usually so diversified that it is simply too much effort to onboard all the suppliers.” Thus, allowing each maturation path to evolve independently is a sensible feature of the model. VII. DISCUSSION A. IMPLICATIONS FOR RESEARCH Our results show that launching a DPP requires more from companies than simply linking a data set to a data carrier (e.g., QR code). This study is the first to characterize and structure the data management capabilities required for launching and operating DPPs. While the model is based on well-established concepts of data management and IT governance, the contribution of our work lies in adapting and applying these concepts to the unique challenges of DPP implementation. As research on DPPs is still in its early stages, the model’s novelty primarily stems from the lack of existing alternatives. Nevertheless, it is grounded in expert input and iterative refinement, ensuring practical relevance. Using the theoretical lens of organizational change and following the design science research, we define and operationalize nine possible paths for improvement that industrial organizations, especially economic operators, will have to follow to become successful with DPPs. Our work also links to the existing research into data sharing and, in particular, the sharing of sustainability-related data [41], [93]. Given the difficulties in inter-organizational data sharing [15], DPPs can address the existing difficulties and ensure that industrial organizations’ concerns about sharing of sustainability-related data are reduced [22]. However, economic operators need to develop data management capabilities to align the supply chain and build trust in data sharing through DPPs. Our work, therefore, builds on previous work on the enablers for data sharing [9], [94], extending it and contextualizing it for the uptake of DPPs. The maturation paths identified in the literature and evaluated by industry experts, who are naturalistic users of the mode, show that automating the data supply of the DPP from source systems is of crucial importance in order to be able to create and handle DPPs on the level of individual products. Although a DPP contains fewer and more static data points than a digital twin, data handling for DPPs is a challenge, especially for complex products made up of different modules. In this context, our work also shows the need to adapt data platforms for DPPs. This finding from our work suggests that more research into data platforms and the workflows they enable is needed in the future. It remains open whether existing infrastructural solutions (e.g., Azure DT or AWS IoT Twinmaker) can also be used for the DPPs or whether new solutions (e.g., Siemens) need to be designed [43], [81], [95], [96]. This opens up opportunities for further design science research projects, especially as DPPs can change the IT landscape and affect actors within and across supply chains [21]. Interestingly, neither the literature nor the experts suggested much about data analytics or artificial intelligence capabilities in the context of a DPP. As data management can help an organization build sufficient resources to use artificial intelligence [9], we expect similar benefits from systematically improving data management by following the model. For instance, the number of data points, such as the battery passport, suggests that the use of AI and data analytics in DPPs may play an important role in making sense of the numerous combinations and volumes of data from a variety of DPPs to optimize product designs or the supply chain and thus gain a competitive advantage [11], [97]. Our maturity model provides decisive support for this scenario. However, the adoption of DPPs beyond the legal minimum will depend on the trust between the supply chain actors. In this respect, our model draws attention to the fact that in order to realize a smart CE beyond the boundaries of one’s own company, further mechanisms for promoting mutual trust and This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. We propose the use of boundary object theory [98], as this theory has already been adapted for other technologies such as digital platforms [99]. Lastly, our work offers minor contributions to the nascent research stream on twin transformation [100]. In particular, the dimensions related to data management and supply chain integration indicate that the adoption of DPPs poses a twin transformation for industrial organizations. In addition, data supply maturity paths go hand in hand with the transformation of economic actors and supply chain players toward greater sustainability. Therefore, our maturity model complements the research of [100] and details the dual transformation through nine multi-level paths. B. IMPLICATIONS FOR PRACTICE As our interviews confirmed, industrial organizations are already coping with the prototypical launches of DPPs. Considering this, the proposed maturity model and the web- based prototype offer a self-assessment tool designed to facilitate the systematic launch of DPPs to generate value beyond regulatory compliance. Although a full-featured service is beyond the study’s scope, the prototype lays the groundwork for future online services that could help industrial organizations assess the current state of their data management, plan targeted improvements, and anticipate the consequences of their choices to follow those paths. The designed model allows us to formulate three propositions to assist decision-makers in practice. First, in order to operate DPPs successfully, industrial organizations need to establish a data governance strategy, especially focusing the efforts on data standardization [23], [78], which not only simplifies system integration but also fosters trust between partners who are reluctant to share product information. After adopting generic and open data formats, adopting or joining industry-wide data standardization consortia can further support collaboration across supply chain tiers [11]. Second, to avoid error-prone workflows, industrial organizations should automate the processes related to data ingestion and control, which would help to leverage more advanced use cases [54]. Third, industrial organizations should invest in scalable, well-governed data platforms [43], [83] that help to manage DPPs, data mapping, metadata, and access rights. Because corporate IT acts as an enabler - supporting business processes and models rather than directly generating revenue - it can be difficult for organizations to balance the costs and benefits of IT investments. This is especially true when there is no clear way to calculate the direct impact of IT spending on business outcomes. The challenge is even greater for organizations that are not primarily driven by data or software, such as industrial organizations, because the increasing impact of data can even create tensions in organizational identity. IT capabilities may be lacking in such cases, as IT is not part of the core business. This can make it difficult to implement a state-of-the-art data architecture. A regulatory push to modernize the enterprise IT landscape could, therefore, help drive innovation by bringing organizations up to current architectural standards. This will create a foundation for developing business capabilities - such as having sufficient infrastructure to use artificial intelligence for data analysis - without having to justify the investment strictly in terms of immediate results [7]. As a result, organizations can not only meet regulatory requirements but also gain the opportunity to implement new, data-driven business models and processes. Our model can serve as a tool to unlock this potential, acting as a guide to identify which components of a data platform could be updated and what a “next level” architecture might look like. The workshops confirmed the applicability of the model to the three domains. Participants noted that, while legal obligations can accelerate progress through certain stages, they can also create a compliance-focused baseline that discourages companies from exceeding minimum requirements. At the same time, the value of voluntary DPP initiatives was recognized, particularly in terms of how the model helps to break down the required capabilities and clarifies the corresponding added value of a DPP. Thus, our maturity model makes the path to a robust DPP transparent for industrial organizations and creates a boundary object for communication between management, IT, the departments responsible for the products for which the DPP is to be introduced, and the supply chain actors who need to be convinced to share their product data with the economic operator. Therefore, in line with the empirical evaluation, we are confident that the DPP maturity model helps decision- makers to reflect on the peculiarities of a DPP implementation, which is a complex decision problem, and master it systematically. They serve as an additional roadmap and extend the findings from [11] and [22] on launching DPPs. Hence, the model aims to support decision-makers in turning the legal obligation to launch DPPs into a competitive advantage, the key to which lies in the development of data architectures and data management capabilities. Nevertheless, its applicability depends on situational factors such as limited IT resources, difficulties of integration with legacy systems, a position within the supply chain, the value of the product, and any relevant legal requirements. C. LIMITATIONS AND FUTURE RESEARCH Our work is subject to some limitations that need to be considered. Although experts did not suggest model extensions during evaluation, future DPP implementations may necessitate new dimensions as they become more concrete in terms of implementation. Although it is necessary to check for the completeness of maturity models [34], we This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. # Page 17^page=17
4 VOLUME XX, 2017 cannot guarantee it due to the early state of the DPP advancement. The legal requirements may change [11], [44], or technological breakthroughs in the realization of DPPs may require new maturation paths for the successful operation of DPPs. It cannot be ruled out that a dominant design in terms of data storage and decentralization will prevail. In addition, our study may reflect a regional bias. In other parts of the world, particularly outside the European Union, where the importance of trustworthy sustainability data and data protection varies, the model may need to be adapted. This provides opportunities for future studies to test the model in different geographical and regulatory environments. However, the capabilities contained in the maturity model are robust enough to meet this equifinality in the design of the DPPs. In addition, our evaluation does not indicate any missing dimensions, and the maturity model can potentially be expanded. Thus, we encourage future decision-makers and researchers to expand it if necessary and test our model by applying it to the emerging DPP ecosystems. Following this, we hope for further domain-specific validation of the model through longitudinal case studies. Moreover, the maturity model is not equally applicable to all companies. Experts note that higher levels may be unmanageable for SMEs and tier-n supply chain actors, limiting the model’s generalizability. Acknowledging this, the model primarily intends to support economic operators in domains where the DPP will become mandatory. Nonetheless, the model also demonstrates how the DPP can be used beyond legal compliance. However, experts noted that it is still a future scenario that will require significant investment, meaning that higher degrees of maturity are currently hardly being sought. This will change with time, positively increasing the utility of the model. However, it is clear that in practice, there are sometimes different understandings of how to implement the legal requirements for launching the DPPs. Although it has been a challenge to understand how these different understandings have arisen, through the initiation of three design cycles and an external evaluation we are confident that our maturity model can assist practitioners to successfully launch the DPPs, operate them purposeful with the digital twins, and use the data infrastructures such as the data platforms for successful data management. VIII. CONCLUSION To answer the RQ, we have designed a maturity model consisting of three dimensions and nine maturation paths. Interactive transfer media additionally supports the dissemination of the maturity model in practice. The model primarily aims to aid industrial organizations considered economic operators and need to launch DPPs. For these organizations, the designed model helps to perform a self- assessment and reflect on ways to improve the design of the DPP and the related data platform as well as the capabilities related to data management and integration of external actors. Digital service providers supporting the launch of a DPP for industrial organizations can also benefit from the maturity model. However, given sufficient technical knowledge, the maturity model can also help industrial organizations to benchmark the service providers with market-ready DPP solutions. Furthermore, workshops with representatives from the plastics and machine tooling industries clarified that the model can be used in domains without a regulatory obligation to launch DPPs. In such domains, organizations embedded in multi-tier supply chains can use the model to evaluate third- party DPP solutions, helping them to make better-informed decisions about whether or not to trust these solutions and share product lifecycle data. From a broader perspective, the maturity model informs stakeholders of the added value that can be achieved through the successful implementation of the DPP in sustainability efforts, such as ESG reporting and the circular economy [6], [37], [12], and helps them to determine the most appropriate configuration. The model also emphasizes the equifinality in DPP design. Although this represents a business opportunity for digital service providers, regulators should observe whether equifinality facilitates the launch of DPPs or if more precise requirements and standardization support, as demonstrated in references [69], [70], are purposeful to align various stakeholders in the collaborative sourcing of DPPs with data. 8 VOLUME XX, 2017 DIMITRI PETRIK is a junior research group leader at the Graduate School of Excellence Advanced Manufacturing Engineering (GSaME) and a postdoctoral researcher at the Department for Information Systems II at the University of Stuttgart. His research concentrates on digital platforms, digital product passports, data spaces, and the use of data and digital technologies to enable smart circular economy. His work has been published in the Information Systems Management Journal, Schmalenbach Business Review, Computers & Industrial Engineering, and the proceedings of the International Conference on Information Systems. He also serves as an organising committee member of the International Conference on Software Business (ICSOB) and the International Workshop on Software- intensive Business (IWSIB) and is a management committee member of the Software Product Management (WI-PrdM) Division of the German Informatics Society. FABIAN DZIERZAWA is a scientific assistant and doctoral researcher at the Department of Information Systems II at the University of Stuttgart. He is also an industrial researcher within the Industrial DataLab, a strategic scientific cooperation between Bosch and the University of Stuttgart. He received his Master of Science in Information Systems from the University of Stuttgart in 2023. Since then, he has been researching methods and approaches for ensuring data quality, launching data products, and transforming organizations toward data ecosystems. His doctoral thesis is conducted as part of the Industrial DataLab collaboration. His research has been published in the proceedings of the Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences. KIRSTEN WARTHMANN received a Bachelor of Science degree in Technically Oriented Business Administration from the University of Stuttgart in 2024. During her studies, she focused on subjects such as the management of business information systems, strategic and international management and organization, as well as specializations such as the automotive fundamentals or the knowledge and information management in manufacturing.
